name: Suspicious DNS Activity
title: DGA-like DNS queries from {host}
description: Host {host} issued a series of DNS queries for random-looking domains ({dga_domain}, {dga_domain2}, ...) with mostly NXDOMAIN responses — a pattern typical of domain generation algorithms used by malware to locate C2 infrastructure.
objective: Determine whether the random-looking DNS queries from {host} indicate a DGA-equipped malware infection, and identify the responsible process.
checklist: Run the detection query and review the queried domain names
Assess the domains: entropy, TLDs, and NXDOMAIN ratio
Identify the process issuing the queries
Check whether any query resolved successfully — a resolution means live C2 contact
Look for network connections following the successful resolution
hints: Human-typed and CDN domains are pronounceable; DGA output like {dga_domain} is high-entropy consonant soup on cheap TLDs (.top, .xyz).
Most DGA queries fail (NXDOMAIN) because the attacker only registers a few of each day's candidates — the failures ARE the signal.
One query resolved. Find it, note the IP it returned, and check firewall logs for a connection to that IP.
expectedEvidence: Series of high-entropy domain queries from a single process
High NXDOMAIN ratio across the queries
One successful resolution followed by an outbound connection
Responsible process is not a browser or known software updater
explanation: This is DGA behavior. Malware families use domain generation so defenders can't just block a static C2 domain — the implant computes hundreds of candidate domains per day and queries them until one resolves (the one the operator bothered to register). The detection signature is statistical: high-entropy names, disposable TLDs, and a burst of NXDOMAINs punctuated by rare successes. The successful resolution followed by an outbound connection confirms live C2. Note what it is NOT: browsers pre-fetching, antivirus cloud lookups, and Chrome's random-subdomain DNS probes (three queries at startup) are common benign lookalikes — process attribution separates them.
reportTemplate: 1. Isolate {host}. 2. Block the resolved domain and {c2_ip} at DNS/egress. 3. Capture the querying binary for analysis. 4. Hunt fleet-wide for queries matching the same pattern.

events.message: action=allowed proto=tcp src={src_ip} dst={c2_ip} dport=443 bytes_out=1204
